narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 49.8% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is just over the threshold we use for approval. As a female Native American student from a middle-income family planning to attend full-time, you bring a strong LSAT score of 44—well above the typical 36.7 average—that stands out positively, along with an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, slightly below the average of 3.21. However, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom tenth of your peers based on grades, had the strongest influence on the model's decision, nudging it toward predicting failure more than any other factor. Right behind that was your Year 3 decile of 1, again in the bottom tenth compared to the average around the middle of the pack, reinforcing the concern about sustained academic performance. Your middle family income bracket came next in influence, followed closely by your Native American background, which is less common in the dataset where about 84% are White. Even your impressive LSAT and undergraduate GPA played supporting roles in tipping the scale toward failure in the model's view for your specific profile, while your gender and full-time status had the least impact. Overall, these factors together painted a picture riskier than typical students, who tend to have mid-range deciles, higher GPAs on average, and more common demographic profiles. Compared to peers, your low early and later law school deciles were particularly standout red flags against your otherwise solid test scores. To improve your future bar exam odds, focus on building stronger study habits and academic consistency now, perhaps through targeted tutoring or coursework to boost any adjustable metrics like GPA before reapplying. Retaking the LSAT isn't urgent given your high score, but excelling in preview law-related classes could help demonstrate potential. Seeking mentorship from successful Native American lawyers might also address underrepresented group challenges indirectly. With dedication to these steps, you can shift the model's view in your favor next time. Keep pushing—you have real strengths to build on.
